agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing interacts with cancellation, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment from a customer to purchase goods or services, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing the commitment. Subject to lifecycle constraints."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document issued to lock in pricing and record the obligation to pay. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Represents a point-of-no-return in the PM's cancellation policy."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to within 1 hour of placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance defines this as a hard financial integrity constraint. Cancelling an invoiced order without a formal reversal process (credit note, void) would corrupt the financial ledger.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, a cancellation request must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different lifecycle stage.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is immediately created and associated with the order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says this should succeed (pre-shipment). BR-2 + BR-3 say this must fail (already invoiced at placement). Cannot define expected behavior until contradiction is resolved."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"If invoicing is asynchronous or can fail, a brief window exists where the order is uninvoiced and cancellable under all three rules. This race condition could be exploited or could cause inconsistent behavior."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether 'before it ships' means before ANY item ships or before ALL items ship. Not addressed in current requirements."},{"scenario":"Cancellation with refund/credit note","expected_behavior":"Finance may allow cancellation if paired with a credit note that formally reverses the invoice, but this is not stated. If so, BR-2 may not actually conflict — but the current wording says invoicing is irreversible, not that the order is irreversible."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If BR-1 wins: invoiced orders get cancelled, creating orphaned invoices or requiring credit notes — accounting complexity and potential revenue recognition issues. If BR-2+BR-3 win: customers cannot cancel any order, leading to chargebacks, refund requests, and customer churn.","operational":"The contradiction makes implementation impossible without a decision. Any choice will break one team's workflow. Operations invoices early to lock pricing; if cancellation is allowed, pricing lock is undermined or credit note workflows are needed.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). If cancellation is blocked immediately after placement, customer satisfaction and trust will suffer significantly.","risk":"Shipping a feature with an unresolved contradiction guarantees either financial ledger corruption (if invoiced orders are cancelled without proper reversal) or customer-facing failures (if all cancellations are blocked). Legal/regulatory risk if invoices are voided improperly.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — their ability to cancel orders depends on resolution. Blocking all cancellations is a severe UX regression."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Invoice integrity is their core concern. Any solution that cancels invoiced orders without a formal reversal mechanism (credit note) violates their accounting controls."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Early invoicing locks pricing, which is their stated goal. If invoicing is deferred to enable cancellation, they lose pricing lock guarantees."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is blocked. Will need clear policies regardless of resolution."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Cannot implement until the contradiction is resolved. Any assumption they make will be wrong for at least one stakeholder."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction that makes them mutually unsatisfiable as stated. BR-1 permits cancellation pre-shipment. BR-2 forbids cancellation post-invoice. BR-3 makes every placed order immediately invoiced. Therefore: every placed order is immediately invoiced, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, yet orders should be cancellable before shipment. This is a P0 contradiction that must be resolved by stakeholders before any acceptance criteria can be defined.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation","Invoice integrity is a hard financial constraint","The three rules as written are mutually exclusive"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept cancellation paired with a credit note (not stated)","Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing with a pricing lock mechanism","Whether 'invoicing is irreversible' means the invoice document or the order's cancellability"],"assumptions":["Invoicing happens synchronously at placement with no delay","No credit note or invoice voiding mechanism currently exists","'Invoiced' means a real financial invoice, not a pro-forma or quote"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. (1) PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. (2) Finance says invoiced orders are never cancellable. (3) Operations says all orders are invoiced at placement. Combining these: no order can ever be cancelled, which directly contradicts requirement (1). The system cannot satisfy all three rules simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Option A: Defer invoicing until shipment — allows cancellation pre-shipment (satisfies PM), but Operations loses pricing lock at placement.","Option B: Keep early invoicing but introduce a credit note / invoice voiding mechanism — allows cancellation of invoiced orders via formal financial reversal (satisfies PM and Operations, requires Finance to accept a reversal workflow).","Option C: Restrict cancellation to a short grace period before invoicing completes — a compromise but requires invoicing to be asynchronous with a defined delay.","Option D: Accept that orders cannot be cancelled post-placement — satisfies Finance and Operations but contradicts the PM requirement and industry norms."],"recommendation":"Option B is the most business-aligned resolution. Keep early invoicing for pricing lock (Operations satisfied), but introduce a formal credit note mechanism that allows order cancellation by properly reversing the invoice (Finance integrity maintained through audit trail). This preserves the PM's cancellation policy while respecting financial controls. However, this requires Finance to agree that 'irreversible' applies to the invoice document itself (it remains on record) while the order's financial obligation is reversed via credit note."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are internally contradictory and cannot be implemented as-is.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation allowed pre-shipment) is impossible to satisfy when BR-2 (no cancellation post-invoice) and BR-3 (invoice at placement) are both enforced. The conjunction of BR-2 ∧ BR-3 implies BR-1 is always false."}]}
